posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

I’m not a scientist, I just know what I sense. But it could just be the palate gets so overwhelmed by the other flavors. What’s your take on premox? Don’t just drive by the thread and take pot shots, at least add your own opinion!

My take on premox? It most certainly exists, but oxidation is oxidation - there are no different kinds of oxidations, because it’s all just oxygen doing its thing on the chemical compounds in wine.

What differentiates premox from normal oxidation is how it appears in a wine. If a wine that is supposed to age wonderfully for 30 years, but only some bottles start randomly showing signs of oxidation, that’s premox. If all the bottles show more or less identical signs of oxidation, it’s not premox but something very different in that particular vintage - and it might be either something from the vineyard (ie. fruit lacking something that is normally there in other vintages), something from the winery (ie. too little SO2; too much dissolved oxygen during the vinification or bottling process) or something wrong with the bottle (ie. a run of faulty corks that have permitted too much oxygen in the wine).

What oxygen does is oxidizes compounds that give the wine its color (making it turn darker and yellow), oxidizes aroma compounds (make them smell and taste different) and oxidizes alcohol into acetaldehyde (making the wine smell aldehydic, which is the main characteristic what people notice when they smell oxidized wine). Oxygen also depletes free SO2 as it produces acetaldehyde, which in turn gets bound with free SO2. However, oxygen doesn’t really do anything with acidity. Some molecules make new compounds with acids over time, but the effect of this on the wine’s acidity is negligible. The level of acidity in a wine stays quite constant over its lifetime.

It doesn’t matter when this oxidation happens - way too early or with decades of aging - it’s still oxidation of wine. Premox is only the specific phenomenon when some bottles randomly get oxidized while others don’t, without any obvious outside influence (ie. too warm temperature, a single faulty cork etc.).

But whatever happens to a wine as it oxidizes, its acidity stays constant. Certain changes might make the acidity feel differently compared to a youthful wine, but usually these changes are very minor - and if anything, I feel the perception of acidity only gets slightly accentuated as the wine loses its fruit and becomes lifeless and flat.

Don_Cornwell

Jun '22

I recently updated the DIAM producer list on the Oxidized Burgundies wiki site, start [Oxidized Burgundies] which I have reproduced below. This update includes additional information that was provided in Burghound (Allen Meadows), Inside Burgundy (Jasper Morris) and Burgundy Report (Bill Nanson).

LIST OF WHITE BURGUNDY/CHABLIS PRODUCERS USING DIAM FOR SUBSTANTIALLY ALL BOTTLINGS (and vintage of inception of complete/relatively complete use):

(Note: With rare exceptions, I have listed below only those producers who use DIAM for all (or nearly all) of their portfolio of wines. Several other [not-listed] producers utilize DIAM for their lower range wines).

Roger Belland (2009)

Daniel Dampt (2014) (90% of production. Remainder under screwcap or natural cork, depending on the importer’s preference)
Sebastian Dampt (2015?)
Vincent Dampt (2015?)
de Bellene and Roche de Bellene (2011)
Droin (2011)
William Fevre (2010)

Hubert Lamy (2017 for 80% of production; 2019 for 100% of production)

Olivier Leflaive (2012)
Benjamin Leroux (2020) [Note: only 20 ppm free SO2 with Diam 30]
Long-Depaquit (Bichot) (2018)
Christian Moreau (2017) (Excludes Clos des Hospices which continues to be closed with cork)
Louis Moreau [Chablis] (2016)
Montille (also Deux Montille and Maison Montille) (2009)
Meurgey-Croses (2019)

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

Did anyone believe in this?

Not that these bottles were premoxed. this has all been discussed somewhere before, but I think these bottles that initially appeared premoxed were actually just really reductive. The bottles would be closed, very little on the nose, nutty, musty (in a non-TCA way), and darker than normal, but had no sherry/baked or cooked apple true oxidized notes. I would often have to stop people from pouring them down the drain. I learned to check very carefully for true oxidative notes, and if not there, be very patient. If set aside for 1-3 hours they would wake up, turn a lighter color, and improve drastically. I most often encountered this with Dauvissat, but that was probably because I had quit buying other white burgs and didn’t have much else. I haven’t had a bottle like this for several years even though I continue to drink my Dauvissat. Now, if a bottle of Dauvissat is off, it’s always actually promoxed.

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked:
Hi Andrew,

if that is the case, how come it is not happening with wines that are sealed with alternative closure? It is not just a Burgundy problem btw. I’d be interested if anyone has experienced an oxidised wine that is 7 years or younger, that was sealed under Diam or screwcap?

Peoples definition of premox certainly varies, but what we are dealing with is oxidation, it is what is deemed as premature that is what is up for debate. The proper aging curve of any wine dictates that there is long and slow oxidation over a period of time and those characters of brulee, honey, mushroom etc add desirable complexity. Some people draw the line at 7 years or 10 years and are happy to accept that anything older that has too much oxidation is too old. My take is, that if a bottle can show well at 25 or 30+ years and the same wine from the same case has that much variation that it is too oxidised to drink, is this not oxidising prematurely? We know the wine can and will age for 30+ years but we are in the lap of the gods so far as the perfect piece of bark is concerned.

regards
Jeremy

posts.name: Otto Forsberg
posts.cooked:
Andrew_K:

Could be. Could also be that older wines that are oxidized just have other aging characteristics that make them taste different from younger, oxidized wines.

You are definitely on to something here.

You see, a wine that is too old for its good, ie. oxidized with age, has also those aged tertiary qualities underneath. A wine’s smell (and, consequently, its flavors) is not a simple reduction of a number of these chemicals resulting in these aromas; it is a matrix in which a certain molecule might have a disproportionately big or small impact for its amount in the wine. Or you might have molecules A and B in such amounts that they remain below their perception thresholds; however, when they are together in the same solution, one of them might actually be perceived: for example molecule A might cancel out effects of molecule C that mask away aromas and/or flavors from molecule B so that you can actually detect aromas and flavors that molecule B is responsible of, even though it should be below detection threshold. Or they could make aromas / flavors of completely unrelated molecule D appear. The way how wine’s flavor matrix works in very unpredictable and that is why wines are so complex and interesting.

With age not only do the different flavor and color molecules and ethanol get oxidized, but also slowly all kinds of different molecules are formed. For example sotolon - the “aged aroma compound” responsible of the nutty, caramel and curry-like aromas of tawny ports, vin jaunes, sherries etc. - takes a lot of time to develop. There is simply no time for sotolon to develop in a young premoxed wine. This is just one of many examples how an old, oxidized wine might have a very different (and often more enjoyable) aroma and flavor profile compared to that of a young premoxed wine, which might have all of its youthful fruit aromas oxidized to oblivion without showing any aged tertiary aromas to, in a sense, give support to the sharp, oxidized aromas of acetaldehyde. Although acetaldehyde is a very powerful aroma compound, often overwhelming most other aromas, the flavor matrix of an old, oxidized wine is very different from that of a young premoxed wine: you have tons of different elements that can give a lot more underlying complexity in an older wine, whereas a young wine’s aroma matrix might be just flat apart from a huge spike from acetaldehyde. This means both these wines smell aldehydic, but in wildly different ways.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
Knut_Sogner:

I am infamiliar with this. I remember reading Jasper Morris’ article with total disbelief. I never read anyone else experiencing this. Nor have I ever noticed what you descibe in the sense of wines changing colour markedly with air.

I have enormous respect for Jasper, but it doesn’t seem plausible to me. I also don’t think highly experienced people would mistake reduction for oxidation. They are not at all similar.

posts.name: Doug Schulman
posts.cooked:
By the way, Jasper wrote about this supposed reversal of premox here:

World Of Fine Wine – 11 Nov 15

White Burgundy out of the Woods?

Exploring the imperfectly understood incidence in recent years of unduly advanced, sometimes completely oxidized bottles of wine - especially white Burgundy.

Est. reading time: 30 minutes

And it was discussed here:

Premox'd White Burgs Turn Great? (per Jasper Morris) WINE TALK
One of the most surprising things I heard in the recent IDTT podcast was Morris’ claim that many bottles from 1996 white Burgundy (from his cellar) are turning out to be good aged white burgs, tasting as expected. These are the same bottlings/crus that when opened years earlier were horribly premoxed. He surmised that there was some character of premature oxidation such that bottles, if kept long enough, could get past. In effect, they’d become nicely aged, and no longer oxidized. Admittedly he…

link_counts.title: Premox'd White Burgs Turn Great? (per Jasper Morris)

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Thanks, Doug, for reminding us that this topic has been discussed extensively before. It’s very much worth re-reading the old thread.

With regard to the reductive vs oxidation thing, I call these faux oxidized wines that wake up with an hour or two of air “reductive”. Maybe that’s the wrong term, but they certainly don’t behave like an ordinary premoxed wine, which get steadily worse the more air that they are exposed to.

Lastly, I think when this topic is discussed, it often seems like folks are talking about two different phenomena–one when a bottle that appears oxidized reverts to a healthy bottle with an hour or two of air, and the other dealing with bottles that might be quite oxidized reverting later in life (weeks,months, years) to a healthy bottle.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: What we see with premox is oxidation in a young wine, vs the more natural oxidation we encounter in senile wines. It’s analogous to leaving a glass of young wine on the counter for a few days. The aromatic integration of the oxidation is very different because the background is very different, if that makes sense. But it is, chemically, the same phenomenon, just in different circumstances.

posts.cooked:
Dav3_Dyr0ff:

Regarding the “rebirth” phenomenon mentioned in a few posts, I haven’t seen the Jasper article many are referring to, but this sounds a lot like the technique Dave Ramey uses for chardonnay and has described (I think I’ve heard it on IDTT and/or the Bedrock podcasts). With him, the “death” and “rebirth” both happen before bottling, so maybe it’s not the same thing but the descriptions in this thread sure sound similar to what he describes in the podcast interview(s) I’ve heard.

Just like there is a premox thread here with comprehensive answers, there’s a brown Chardonnay thread where the process is well explained. It’s a fairly common practice. In short, the compounds that oxidize then fall out of solution, leaving a wine less vulnerable to oxygen.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Both Boillot and Ramonet still get a fair amount of love… despite being two of the worst premox offenders

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked:
Wes_Barton:

Just like there is a premox thread here with comprehensive answers, there’s a brown Chardonnay thread where the process is well explained. It’s a fairly common practice. In short, the compounds that oxidize then fall out of solution, leaving a wine less vulnerable to oxygen.

Could you please make all of the oxidized compounds in my Dauvissats fall out of solution? That would be great. Thanks!

posts.name: Uli K ł ū m p p
posts.cooked: Agreed, best to listen to David Ramey at length. If you want a pointer, he explains his journey around oxidation starting at 4:20 min in IDTT Wine 468: David Ramey and the Evolution of Chardonnay | I'll Drink to That! Wine Talk. Zelma Long and a filter sales rep were among his inspirations…

link_counts.title: IDTT Wine 468: David Ramey and the Evolution of Chardonnay | I'll Drink to That! Wine Talk

posts.name: Nick Ryan
posts.cooked: May as well bump this thread instead of creating yet another one about premox… going to start a white Burg collection. Is the strat really just “stick to DIAM producers”? The list on the wiki has some impressive names but still excludes an enormous number. I know the first post here is a data point – are there more data points that show this is still a worrisome issue with the holdouts?

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Stick to DIAM if you want to drink the wine at more than three years old. Too much risk with cork even if some producers have done much better than others.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: There are producers with good track records with cork whom you can confidently buy.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Examples? PYCM isn’t too bad, if not perfect, Coche-Dury isn’t too bad, if not perfect, but in general it needs a lot more confidence than I possess. Some of the very best names have been very unreliable indeed. I’m pleased to hear that Roulot has at last switched to DIAM after all the problems, and we should take heart from the success at places like Leflaive and Jadot.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: PYCM and Coche indeed have never had any sort of wide-spread premox issues, so I buy with confidence. Paul Pillot, Moreau are others. Of course “best names” doesn’t mean no premox - ehem Ramonet.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Pillot and Moreau have not always lasted well over the medium term, sadly. I don’t love DIAM but it does appear to be the only solution unless one goes down the screwcap route, which is a big divergence.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Oxidation is always possible over time with the variability of cork and especially storage/transport conditions. We had two mags of 91/95 Pillot last weekend that were oxidized, but that doesn’t necessarily indicate premox.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Certainly at 34 years old it is ox rather than premox; though I always wonder how wines from the 1970s have lasted so well-massive yields, underripeness and tons of sulphur are a large part of the answer.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Under-ripeness definitely isn’t part of the answer. All the celebrated vintages back then were the ripe ones, not the tart ones like today, and when you ask any of the old-timers how they decided when to pick, they will say words to the effect of, “when the grapes were golden, dummy”. The under-ripe vintages of the 1970s mostly make for pretty unfriendly drinking today.

posts.name: Nick Gangas
posts.cooked: I’ve never had a bad Raveneau

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: Underripeness by today’s standards is what I had meant, but I do recall successful mostly rather ungrand wines from 1970, 1973,1974 and 1977 among non-appreciated vintages for which friendliness would certainly not be the immediate descriptor of choice but were very much worth drinking. Assuming good corks their longevity can be astonishing.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Today, the norm is to pick fruit a lot greener, visually, than in the good vintages of the 1970s (I’d co-opt 1973 from your list and leave you the rest). Overall growers today are picking earlier in the vines’ cycle, with less skin-maturity, than in the celebrated vintages of the 1970s and 1980s. And even in the less favorable vintages, green-ish fruit picked in October, at the end of the vines’ cycle, isn’t the same as green fruit picked in August in terms of phenolic development. Just as red wines from unripe (idem overripe) grapes have a short life expectancy, so do whites.

posts.name: Marcu$ Stanley
posts.cooked: This is very interesting. I like the old school style of rich buttery white Burgundy (of course with underlying structure and acidity too). When I want Chablis I buy Chablis; my favorite white Burgs are rich but still unmistakably Burgundy. Any suggestions for producers who still offer this?

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: PYCM has had a lot of issues with the Corton Charlemagne (around 30% in my experience) and Chevalier Montrachet (around 20%). My experience may be an outlier, but PYCM is far from a “safe bet”.

posts.name: Ąņɗŗęŵ Ķļϋǥ
posts.cooked: Interesting. I’ve had a bunch of CC and nary a one. One of the things I believe about premox is that anything that increases aging will exponentially affect a wine that has the potential for premox. So the same bottle with perfect provenance, cold cellar, never moved might show ok, but if it has transport or storage issues, it’s more likely to fall over the premox cliff. Which could also be why people seem to find lower instances in France than elsewhere in the world.

posts.name: Nick Ryan
posts.cooked:
Interesting. If transport and movement is indeed a big factor that could explain to a large degree why so many still haven’t moved away from the traditional cork. The USA (and Asia to a lesser degree) is a large market but surely Europe is still by far the largest for Burgundy.

Also, while browsing white Burgs to backfill I’m anecdotally noticing that they are imported to the USA to a far lesser degree than the reds. The nosebleeds with gigantic opportunities for margin like DRC and Coche of course are available but vast swaths of well-regarded producers in the $75-$300 range, including many poured at La Paulee, are complete no-shows. (I wonder if an argument can be made it’s much harder to make “Burgundian” Pinot Noir in the US than Burgundian Chardonnay, but that’s probably a topic for another thread.)

posts.name: Larry Link
posts.cooked:
Rauno_E_NZ:

PYCM has had a lot of issues with the Corton Charlemagne (around 30% in my experience) and Chevalier Montrachet (around 20%). My experience may be an outlier, but PYCM is far from a “safe bet”.

My experience is different. I’ve consumed 228 bottles of PYCM over the years with only 1 bottle that was premoxed (an 05 MP) and 4 that were corked. That includes 26 bottles of the Corton Charlemagne and 6 of the Chevy. You can’t get much better than that.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: That’s a great sample size and success rate :)! Shame your dud was an MP - it’s been the “best” wine from PYCM for me due to quality and consistency. Much smaller sample size, of course.

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: You would certainly have a more complete understanding than do I but I remain intrigued by the longevity of modest wines from bad 1970s vintages.

posts.name: Oliver McCrum
posts.cooked: I would hope anyone shipping fine wines like these is using refrigerated shipping…

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: Arguments regarding the incidence of premox based purely on personal experience are a bit like arguing that taxis never run people over because you have never been run over by a taxi…

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: Sadly, personal experience is all most of us can muster :)! And I think exchange of personal experience and anecdote is not an argument. I found Larry’s experience very useful.

posts.name: Glen Gold
posts.cooked: Also, though you probably have a lifetime of drinking to keep score for, the taxi data point might only be updated once

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
I’m not discounting anecdote - just arguing that positives are more useful than negatives!

Overall, I think you have a few different scenarios:

  • producers who do pretty much everything to make long-lived wines, and bottle with very good quality natural cork and high free SO2. They typically have low incidence of premox, but are not immune
  • producers who by and large have a good track record, but who have had big problems with certain cuvées and vintages, likely due to dissolved oxygen pick up, errors made in adjusting SO2 levels, or (especially) bad batches of corks
  • producers who make short-lived wines that are best drunk young, due to practices and poor quality corks

In terms of the causes, however, it’s a quantitative not a qualitative difference.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked:
Plus the category of “producers who use DIAM” (as category 0).

I was wondering if viticulture practices / fruit can have a major influence, so keen on any thoughts you might have on that, William. For example, I’ve had great (though limited sample size!) experiences with PYCM MP and Boillot Moucheres, which I understand are Domaine wines, while I’ve had much more variable experiences with PYCM CC and Boillot CC, which I think are negoc wines. While I know my personal experience may be misleading to draw any conclusions from, it has made me wonder whether those negoc cuvees are more prone to premox because they are not farmed and harvested to the more exacting standards of the Domaine. Idle speculation of course

posts.name: Sam Kwak
posts.cooked: Or the inverse - me as someone who has been run over by a taxi (true story) telling everyone it is inevitable you will get run over by a taxi at some poont

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Without reference to your particular examples (I don’t know if PYCM farms the Perrières, for example), there are indeed some possible factors that can be linked to viticultural practices including:

  • yield + solid to juice ratio, and resulting redox potential of the wine
  • copper residue on fruit
  • canopy management and the result of sun exposure on skin development
  • level of physiological maturity at harvest
  • and others…

However, in Burgundy, the added factor is that a lot of whites are traded in must (juice), not fruit. Again, I’m talking in general terms not describing these specific cases. But when buying must, it’s generally settled pretty clear (why would you pay for solids you have to discard when you’re paying e.g. 40,000 EUR per barrel of Charlemagne), and will have been pressed following the supplier’s practices (perhaps less extractively) and treated following the supplier’s post-pressing practices (e.g. pectinase enzymes followed by sulfites). So that obviously will impact the finished wine.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: If you jaywalk enough in NYC, it will happen…

posts.name: Wil Raley
posts.cooked: What vintage did Roulot switch to Diam? I thought it was only for the Bourgogne Blanc. I have some 22s that haven’t arrived yet. Sure would be a relief if they were under Diam

posts.name: Tom Blach
posts.cooked: 23,.I think.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
The wikipedia article on premox does a good job of discussing all the issues.

I would just say that when you change one thing in a system you have to think about the whole system. When a lot of things are changed…

posts.name: Brad England
posts.cooked:
Rauno, also find your comments interesting. Like Larry, I drink a fair amount of PYCM (and a fair amount of his CC) and I can’t recall a single premox bottle. Not at all wanting to be argumentative, just a counter data point.
PS, and unrelated. Where are you in NZ? Just had a great week in Queenstown last month and enjoyed the Gibbston vineyard Pinots I tasted.

posts.name: Rauno E N G el
posts.cooked: In Wellington, which is nice, but not as stunning as Queenstown :). And don’t worry, I don’t find the counterpoints argumentative at all. Glad that others are getting highly consistent bottles. Most of the duds I had were “advanced” rather than premoxed, spanning’09s to ‘11s. Bought mostly in HK at the time. Only sporadically bought his CC since, but big fan of the Meursault Perrieres.

posts.name: Mel Knox
posts.cooked:
When I first saw first hand premox I was in denial. Two guys --David page and John Ritchie-- put on a series of amazing tastings that I was lucky enough to attend. At one, six out of eight Montrachet’s were premoxed.

I put it out of mind thinking it was just weird, but it kept happening.

Then I remembered one guy telling me that because he was biodynamic, he could bottle with 15 parts free. It was clear that grapes were coming in riper and riper and yet they were doing the opposite of what was called for, which was more SO2.

My sources then told me people were reverting to their old ways, more SO2, etc.
But still they had problems.

Then I read the article somebody posted here about glutathione. Two researchers in Bordeaux, Lavigne and Dubourdieu ( sadly no longer with us) wrote about how important this was as an anti oxidant. Its role in wine was discovered at Davis in the '80s by Vern Singleton. The authors suggested that lees were weaker in glutathione because of vineyard practices.And, of course, newer presses meant fewer lees.

I first went to Burgundy in 1977. My memory of white wine making involved more SO2, old fashioned Vaslin presses that squeezed the be=Jesus out of the grapes, and more reductive wines. It also involved wines that aged beautifully but maybe you didn’t want to touch them right away,

I took a winemaker–maker of famous whites – to see Dave Ramey when he was at MatanzasCreek–1986??. Dave talked about letting the juice brown etc and explained the whole process. The response?? I always wondered why the wine did that.In other words, Burgundians knew about juice browning. They just never bothered with the scientific explanation.

posts.name: David Bueker
posts.cooked: Dave Ramey - brown juice and DIAM. The wines are fantastic and age very well.

posts.name: Robert Grenley
posts.cooked: My 1996 Ramonet Batard’s and Ruchottes were all premoxed years ago at about 7-10 years of age, so maybe you will have no problem finding premoxed 96 Ramonet’s.
